topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a bright, insistent melody that felt like a physical assault on the quiet hollow in my chest. I stood near the buffet table, nursing a lukewarm glass of champagne, trying to look engaged in the polite chatter, but my eyes kept snagging on the periphery. Then I saw him. He was laughing, a sound I hadn't heard in years, his hair slightly longer, the easy confidence radiating off him like heat. My breath hitched, a tiny, sharp intake that nobody noticed. He turned, and for a split second, the world narrowed down to that single point of recognition. His smile faltered, a flicker of something unreadable crossing his features before he quickly turned back to the conversation. I felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in my stomach, a coldness spreading from my core outward. I gripped my glass so tightly my knuckles turned white, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 2]

It was the forced cheerfulness of the reception that felt like sandpaper against my nerves. Every shared glance, every familiar face, was a deliberate act of endurance. I kept my posture rigid, a defensive shell against the vulnerability I was desperately trying to conceal. Then, across the crowded dance floor, he materialized. He was talking to someone, his movements fluid and effortless. Seeing him brought a sudden, crushing weight down on my shoulders, making it physically difficult to draw a full breath. My gaze locked, and the noise of the party seemed to recede into a dull, distant hum. I wanted to disappear. A tremor ran through my hands, and I instinctively took a step back, bumping into a passing waiter. The brief contact was enough; the sudden proximity to him, even from across the room, sent a wave of intense, painful emptiness washing over me.

[story 3]

I was leaning against a marble pillar, pretending to admire the floral arrangements, the scent of lilies suddenly overwhelming. It was a performance, this social obligation, and I was failing spectacularly. Then, he walked into my line of sight. He stopped near the entrance, and for a moment, time seemed to stretch, slowing to a painful crawl. I watched him, cataloging every subtle shift in his expression—the way his eyes scanned the room, the slight tilt of his head. A tightness constricted my throat, making swallowing feel like an impossible feat. When his eyes met mine, there was a moment of absolute stillness, a recognition so deep it felt like a physical ache behind my ribs. I managed a small, jerky nod, a gesture that felt utterly inadequate, and then I turned away abruptly, needing distance before the internal pressure became unbearable.
